title.en: The Self-Liberation of Existence: The instructions that reveal the own-forms of virtue and vice; pointing-out the Bardo of Existence

source.attribution: A treasure text concealed by Guru Padmasambhava and revealed by the tertön Karma Lingpa (གྲུབ་ཐོབ་ཀརྨ་གླིང་པ)

blocks.en: Having earlier presented the introductions to the signs of death from The Great Liberation through Hearing in the Intermediate States, an extract from the Peaceful and Wrathful Lotus Deities, and having introduced the bardo of reality, the 'great liberation by seeing', now the introduction to the bardo of becoming will be presented.

heading.en: Herein is Contained the Introduction to the Bardo of Becoming

blocks.en: Having completed the previous introductions, here the introduction to the bardo of becoming is presented. The Guru appears in the costume of Yama Dharmaraja:

blocks.bo: བེར་ནག་པོ་གཅིག་གྱོན༔ མགོ་ལ་གླུང་མགོའི་འབག་ཅིག་གོན༔ ལག་ན་ཞགས་པ་ནག་པོ་གཅིག་ཐོགས་ལ་གཡས་སུ་འཁོད༔ འབྱུང་པོ་སྤེའུའི་མགོ༔ སྤྲེའུའི་གདོང་ཅན༔ ལག་ན་བྲེ་སྲང་ཁྱེར་བ༔དུར་ཕག་མགོ་ཅན༔ ལག་ན་ཁྲམ་ཤིང་ཐོགས་པ༔ སྟག་པ་སྒྲུལ་མགོ་སྦྲུལ་གྱི་མགོ་ཅན༔ལག་ན་མེ་ལོང་ཐོགས་པ༔གཏུམ་པོ་དོམ་གྱི་མགོ་ཅན་ལག་ན་སྙུད་པ་ཐོགས་པ༔རྔམ་པ་སེང་མགོ་སེདྨེའི་མགོ་ཅན་ལག་ན་ཐོ་བ་ཐོགས་པ༔འདབ་ཆགས་ཁྱུང་མགོ་ཁྱུང་གི་མགོ་ཅན་ལག་ན་ཀི་ག་ལོང་ར་ཨིགི་ཀལགལ་གསག་།
blocks.en: He wears a single black cloak, and on his head, a single turban of ox-hide. In his right hand he holds a single black noose, and he is seated. The Monkey-headed Elemental Spirit, with a monkey's face, holds scales. The Boar-headed Cemetery Spirit carries a wooden tally. The Snake-headed Demon, with the face of a snake, holds a mirror. The fierce Bear-headed figure, with the face of a bear, holds an arrow. The awesome Lion-headed figure, with the face of a snow lion, holds a hammer. The Bird-headed figure, with the face of a garuḍa, holds a saw. These diverse animal-headed figures, dignified and standing in rows, are arrayed to the right and left of Dharmaraja.

The innate demon wears a single wrathful black turban, a single black garment, and carries a vessel of black pebbles. The innate deity wears a white peaceful mask and a white silk robe, and carries a bowl filled with white pebbles.

Before Dharmaraja, on his right side, a white cotton pathway is laid out like a white path, and at its end, an image of the Great Compassionate One with one face and four arms, adorned with ornaments, is seated on a throne hidden by a curtain. On his left, a black cotton pathway is laid out like a black path, leading to the door of a dark room.

Elsewhere, in his home, a being who has completed his lifespan named the late Srījata ('Gloriously Born') is placed in hiding. In a corner, a deceased being, a wrongdoing outcaste of low birth, named the late Laksanaraka ('Hundred Thousand Hells'), is placed in a woodpile. Dharmaraja draws a tally mark on that wooden slate and declares: "Ox-headed Rakṣa, from a certain lodging in the human world, a person whose time has come has appeared on my tally." So he speaks.

heading.en: ACT ONE: The Judgment of the Sinner Laksanaraka

blocks.en: Ox-headed Rakṣa calls to the Snake-headed Demon: "You, Fierce Snake-headed One, look in your mirror! That deceased being—what is his country? What is his caste? What is his name? Look!" Thus commanded, the Snake-headed Demon replies:

"That deceased being whose time has come: his land is in India, in Zangling. His city is the town of Śānti Nagpo, a township of butchers. As for his caste, among the four castes, he belongs to the outcaste class. His name is called 'Sinful One, Hundred Thousand Hells.' His life's count has fallen from the register onto the tally. Now run and fetch him!"
Thereupon, the Ox-headed Rakṣa, the Boar-headed Cemetery Spirit, and the innate demon—these three—run off. In their wake, the innate deity also rushes to his aid.

From that being's lodging, as soon as they find the sinner, they lasso his neck with the black noose. Ox-head and Pig-head seize his hands and lead him away. The innate demon blows on his black pebble vessel and drives him from behind. Meanwhile, the innate deity places a mere six white pebbles in the white pebble vessel, and, in embarrassment, tries to help his bad companion. Holding a white silk scarf with folded hands, he attempts to petition Ox-headed Rakṣa on behalf of the deceased, but this is unsuccessful. Laksanaraka is brought weeping and wailing into the presence of the king and seated there.

Dharmaraja interrogates him: "Hey! Black man, you who carry scales—who are you? Where have you come from? Why can you not bear to look directly at me? You appear to have obtained a human body. What portion of your white virtuous deeds remains? Have you ever scrupled about committing black evil deeds? What explanation do you have? Speak now, quickly!"

So he asks, and the sinful one replies: "Alas! Alas! I petition before the great Yama Dharmaraja! I obtained a mere human body. My resources were few, my food and clothing poor. I had many female dependants and thus lacked the means to hire workers. I had to take the lives of many beings. Lacking the means to buy grain, for many years my food was nothing but fresh, warm meat. For many years I did not taste rice beer or white liquor; my drink was water and blood.

In that lodging in the human world, there were many who claimed to be Gurus. They frequently taught about the defects of committing negative actions and the benefits of carrying out positive actions. However, I did not go to them. And everyone would say to me: 'Do not do such evil. One day, death will come. At that time you will go to the hells. Therefore, abandon wrongdoing and perform virtuous actions!' So they advised. But in my mind I thought: 'I do not know whether it is true that hells exist. I have never seen anyone return after going to the hells. Where are these hells that supposedly exist? These are just the lies of clever-talking people. Below this earth is just solid earth and solid rock; there are no hells. Above is only empty sky; there are no buddhas. Right now, while alive, if I kill for my food, it is fine. When I die, my body will be taken to the charnel ground, and birds and wild animals will eat it, leaving not a trace. The mind will vanish. So, at that time, who is left to go to the hells? Ha ha!' Thus I spoke. Thinking the hells were not real, I committed evil.

And I thought, 'Even if the hells do exist, they will only be set in some remote future life. So if I can satisfy myself with food and clothing now, the next life doesn't matter.' So, through the power of misunderstanding, lack of awareness, and ignorance, I committed evil. It happened because I was not acquainted with the fact that you, Dharmaraja, you Ox-headed Rakṣa, and all you others actually exist here in this bardo realm. I did not commit evil with the intention of mocking you, although you exist here. The fault lies with this misunderstanding and lack of awareness. So now, lord and entourage, I request you not to pass judgment upon me! If I had known, when I lived in the human world, that this all directly existed, I would never have committed negative actions. Now I am stuck here under the influence of negative mental states. Alas! Alas! Since you are the King of Dharma, be merciful to me! Be my friend and supporter! From here downward, if there is great suffering, Dharmaraja, do not send me down there! Instead, please send me upward into the human world! Thereafter I will never perform evil actions again—only virtuous ones! Kyi-hü kyi-hü!" So he says.

Then the innate deity, offering a white silk scarf, petitions: "O great king, please consider in your heart! This one called 'low-caste chandala of Zangling,' not knowing good and evil, blinded by ignorance, committed evil through the power of ignorance. Since he did not act knowingly or with understanding, please do not decree punishment for him! He has, after all, also carried out a small number of virtuous actions. Once when six people were drowning in a great river, with a virtuous mind he pulled them all from the water. As a sign of this, here are six white pebbles. And besides that, whatever incidental virtue he may have accumulated—great Dharmaraja, I request your forgiveness on his behalf." So saying, he prostrates three times before Dharmaraja.

Then the black demon retorts: "Ha ha! White deity, do you have that much to say? Are you not ashamed to carry this nearly empty bowl? This one called low-caste chandala: his whole life was steeped in sin, and he cut off any share of virtue. He killed every being he saw. He took warm flesh for food. He drank warm blood as a beverage. He continuously spoke harsh words as his ornament. In the upper valleys, he slew innocent wildlife. In the lower valleys, he slew innocent fish. And in between, he beat innocent beggars. He defamed each and every Guru. He set great fire to the temple. He poured great poison into the great ocean. He set vast forests ablaze with fire. He beat his parents and destroyed a sacred reliquary. Now in India, in Zangling, there is no one with greater evil than you. Look at the mound of black pebbles!

"In case you don't recognize yourself: the continent is India, Zangling. The town is Śānti Nagpo. Your father is the butcher Tripon; he passed away years ago and came here. Your mother is the butcheress Pelkyi; she came here five years ago. You yourself are a butcher, a chandala. When did you arrive here? The year is the Water Female Pig year. This year your food-count came to an end. Eastern Indians call you 'Red-handed Butcher.' Southern Indians call you the 'Sinful One, Hundred Thousand Hells.' Western Indians call you 'Black Low-caste Chandala.' Northern Indians call you the 'Black Killer of All.' Your caste is the black low-caste chandala caste. Your lineage is that of wrongdoing butchers. All generations of your family, without exception, are expert guides on the path to hell. When you were killing, you seemed delighted. When you ate, it seemed tasty. But does it taste so good now? What use is your clever, slick-talking tongue now? The debt of countless beings' lives has fallen on you; it is time for you to experience suffering.

If you do not experience this suffering, the multitude of beings will have lost everything, and even a hundred thousand hells would be too good for you. "As for your answer to Dharmaraja: even he, with his mighty stature, cannot stop the effects of your negative actions. From the great act of burning down a temple to the small act of killing a flea—this is the heap of pebbles you have accumulated. So it is best if you prepare yourself to move on. Get ready to go swiftly on this black road! There, the sealed copper cauldron that awaits you is wide and deep. The waves of boiling bronze are fierce. The fiery mass of past actions is intensely hot. Yama's messengers have little compassion. Dharmaraja's face is extremely stern. The weapons of past actions are sharply pointed. The black wind of past actions is very powerful. Now is the time for you to go to such a place!

Though I really do have compassion for you, I am nonetheless very satisfied. You must carry on your back the weight and measure which you used fraudulently. You must wear at your side the weapons with which you killed many sentient beings. There is no denying or misrepresenting these things. Now the time has come for you to go to the citadels of the eighteen hells." So he shouts, clenching his fist.

Then from the mouth of Dharmaraja: "Alas! How pitiful! How terrifying! When one has obtained a human form, one should proceed with highly purposeful virtuous conduct on the path of liberation, where inferior rebirths are left behind. And thus one is never separated from peace and happiness. But on this occasion when you obtained a human life, you threw away positive actions like dirt and seized upon negative actions, for which reason you have wasted this highly meaningful human body, which is difficult to obtain. Since you have done nothing at all, other than return empty-handed, you are only carrying the burden of your negative acts. How pitiable! Since the actions you have carried out must ripen upon yourself, you cannot be protected even by the power of a thousand buddhas. So I cannot respond to this in any way. Since the results of your premeditated negative acts, which you carried out when you had freedom, will all come to ripen upon you, even though you wail and lament, no one should feel sorry for you. Even though you have hopes in me, I have no means to act. This mirror of past actions, luminous with all phenomenal existence, directly shows virtue and evil in their own forms. How can it be that no past actions will appear in the case of wrongdoers?

"When the deity and demon who are your co-emergent consciences compare the black and white pebbles of past actions, happy is the person who has accumulated virtue! How very remorseful are you, who have accumulated evil! On this dangerous, narrow red passageway traversed by all, when you are brought to trial by the executors of Yama's rites, even though you may have been once very powerful, here it will be of no avail. Now is the time for the hearts and lungs of all great wrongdoers to be torn apart. Since you have practised non-virtue, this karmic summons is swifter and more powerful than lightning. You cannot escape by running, and showing remorse will be of no help. How pitiful are the human beings of Jambudvīpa who do not strive in the sacred teachings! There will be no way of helping them.

"This detailed accounting of positive and negative past actions forbids even the most minute negative deeds. So what are those humans who have no scruples thinking? The white path of liberation leads to joy, while the black path of inferior rebirth leads to suffering. Yet the humans of Jambudvīpa do not abandon evil, nor do they accomplish virtue. Even though they may feel sadness and remorse, past actions cannot be altered. Karma follows like the body and its shadow. I hold all rewards in respect of positive virtues, and I exact punishments in respect of negative deeds. Judging the balance and counterbalance, weighing with scales and measures, I examine good and bad actions. So even though you have regrets, I cannot change anything. Now, even though I am very merciful, there is no way I can help you. Once you take the black path to the hell realms, you cannot be saved, even if you were to be engulfed by the compassion of the Sublime Ones. So there is absolutely no way for me to do anything. The Victorious One has said that sentient beings must reap the fruits of their own past actions. So now the Ox-headed One will lead you away! May your evil and obscurations be swiftly purified, and may you ultimately attain the ultimate level of buddhahood." So he speaks.

Ox-headed Rakṣa says: "Since it is your own doing, even though you may have regrets, what is the use? As for us, we are not responsible. We simply distinguish truth from falsehood. Since you have no responsibility for your past actions, it would be meaningless for us to harm you. Now, as this is the outcome of your past actions, be off quickly!" So saying, they lead him with the black noose. The black demon goes behind and drives him, goading him down the path represented by the black carpet. Then, inside the dark room, the sound of beating and killing resounds, and he is made to utter all manner of lamentations.

Then the Guru makes a detailed elucidation, beginning with the words: "O children of my lineage, those who have committed evil deeds will be singled out in this manner, and their sufferings will be the same as those described here. This will certainly happen to us as well. At that time, whether or not this core dilemma besets you will depend entirely on yourselves. Therefore, from now on, it is crucial to strive after virtue and to be cautious of negativity. O children of my lineage…" and so forth, he elaborates extensively.

heading.en: ACT TWO: The Judgment of the Householder Srījata

blocks.en: Then again, Dharmaraja draws a tally mark on that wooden slate and declares: "Hey, Ox-headed Rakṣa! Also, from a certain lodging in the human world, there is a person whose food-count has run out and who has appeared on my tally. Go fetch him!" So he commands.
Ox-headed Rakṣa calls to the Snake-headed Demon: "Hey, Fierce Snake-headed One! Look in your mirror! See where that deceased being is!" Thus commanded, the Snake-headed Demon replies:

"The land of the one whose time has come is in northeast India. The city is called Kāmarūpa. His caste, among the four castes, is the royal caste. His name is the householder Srījata. His lifespan and merit are exhausted, and his food-count has fallen onto the tally. Now run quickly and fetch him!"

So all three—Ox-headed Rakṣa, Boar-headed Cemetery Spirit, and the innate demon—run off. In their wake, the innate deity also rushes to his aid. From that being's lodging in Kāmarūpa, as soon as they find the householder Srījata, the Ox-headed One leads him away and the Boar-headed One seizes him by the hand, while the innate demon drives him from behind. The innate deity offers assistance, and they lead him into the presence of Dharmaraja.

Dharmaraja interrogates him: "O child of my lineage, deceased being, have you come from a lodging in the human world? You seem to have obtained the precious human body of freedom and favorable opportunities, so difficult to find. You have encountered the difficult-to-encounter teachings of the Buddha. You were born in the difficult-to-be-born-in Jambudvīpa. What white virtuous deeds have you accumulated? During your stay in the lodging of the human world, with a pure mind and clean body, did you direct your thoughts to the Dharma? What compounded virtues and offerings do you possess? Did you erect supports of buddha-body, speech and mind? Did you commission the writing and recitation of scriptures? Did you mould tsa-tsa? Did you offer water libations? Did you clear rocks from roads? Did you remove bothersome thorns from dangerous paths? Did you make offerings to those above? Did you bestow charity on those below? Did you scatter barley dough and grain at anthills? What other subtle and fine acts of virtue, over and above these, did you accumulate? Furthermore, did you engage in study, contemplation, and meditation? Did you observe the eight precepts and the fasting precepts? Did you receive meditative instruction, empowerments, and guidance? What other virtues of this kind have you accumulated? Tell me quickly now!

"What black evil deeds have you committed? Through the door of body: from the greatest offence, such as killing one's parents, down to the smallest, such as killing lice and nits, stealing, sexual misconduct and so forth—what negative actions of body have you accumulated? Through the door of speech: meaningless gossip, harsh words, slander, lying and so forth—what negative actions of speech have you accumulated? Through the door of mind: covetousness, harmful intent, distorted views and so on—what negative actions of mind have you accumulated? Also, with regard to the five inexpiable crimes and the five approximate crimes, what non-virtuous and negative actions have you accumulated, motivated through body, speech, or mind? Tell me now in clear detail!" So he asks.

Then that deceased person, in a terrified and trembling voice, replies: "I petition before the presence of Dharmaraja! I am that very person who dwelt in the city of Kāmarūpa in northeast India, the householder named Srījata. I too had dependants. But I was a humble person, self-sufficient in food and drink. I was a faithful devotee who offered fine alms to all the excellent Gurus who came to my district. I encouraged others toward virtue. With a virtuous and good mind myself, I restored temples that had fallen into disrepair. I ransomed many living creatures from certain death. I repeatedly received profound empowerments and guidance. When I travelled, I cleared dangerous paths; when I stayed, I recited the Six-syllable Mantra and observed the eight precepts and fasting precepts. I am a householder who made offerings above to the Three Precious Jewels and gave charity below to those of unfortunate birth. I am indeed a bodhisattva. This is why everyone calls me the householder Laksmin ('Endowed with Wealth'). Such is my unbroken garland of virtue.

"As for my black evil deeds: although I had the intention not to harm even a single living being, since I was submerged in the perverse, confused world of cyclic existence, and I had a son, in order for him to be able to take a wife, I was involved in taking the lives of ten beings for the wedding feast. To confess that evil, I recited the Vajra Cutter one hundred times. Then, when I and my neighbors took the lives of many beings in order to pay taxes in support of the village community, I too felt much remorse and made confession. Also, since I lived as a worldly householder, due to circumstance and nature, I wondered whether beings underfoot or underhand might have died. I felt much remorse about that. Since the credentials of my virtue and evil are established in these ways, I beg you to consider this conduct and favor me with your compassion." So he petitions.

Then Dharmaraja speaks: "Well then, if what you say is true, then you have thought well about virtue and evil, and about cause and effect. However, you humans are skilled at lying. I shall consult the karmic mirror!" He displays the mirror and says: "Ah, look, fortunate child of my lineage! This karmic mirror, luminous with all phenomenal existence, is clearer than the omniscient eye that perceives the three times. As it directly shows the forms of karmic virtue and evil, whatever virtue and evil you committed in the human world, right now, in this very moment, will visibly appear. So how can you lie and deceive? Look now, look into this mirror!" So he commands. When the householder looks into the mirror, he sees that the account of his deeds, as he told them, is not false. "O good man, go along the white path! Let him go!" So he declares.

Then the black demon speaks: "Hey! May I speak in the presence of Dharmaraja! This bad person called the householder Srījata has indulged his whole life in sin and has cared only for his own long-term selfish interests. His name is householder Srījata, but his nickname is 'Monkey-head.' As for this faithless householder Srījata, his birth year is the Iron Ox year. His birthplace is Kāmarūpa city. His background is that of the royal caste, yet he committed evil. Hurrying uptown, he killed his father and quarrelled. Rushing downtown, he fought with his neighbors. He threw stones at the camel belonging to his neighbor Srībhadra, and although he killed it, he denied his action and lied about it. He pushed the scribe Prajñāmati's young elephant over a cliff, and although he killed it, he denied his action and lied about it. Now, let the pebbles be weighed! How will it be possible for him to take the white path? It is utterly fitting that he should go on the black path!" So saying, he pours out about three handfuls of black pebbles into his bowl.

Then the white deity petitions: "I petition before the presence of Dharmaraja! The householder Laksmin Srījata is a most faithful devotee. He has strong interest in the sacred teachings and has been cautious of evil. When about five hundred miscreants were brought to trial in the presence of the Mahārāja, he offered five hundred srang of gold and saved their lives. In the Indian city of Kāmarūpa, the householder Srījata alone is one who possesses great faith. Now, let the pebbles be weighed! What reason is there for him to take the black path? He should now set out, guided along the white path!" So saying, he pours out about six measures of white pebbles into his bowl.

The Monkey-headed Elemental Spirit says: "Deity and demon of conscience, both of you, there is no need to disagree! When I raise my scale balance, the weight of virtue and evil will be definitively determined by the weights. Now, if you look, the outcome will be obvious!" So saying, he weighs the pebbles on the scales, whereupon the white container is three times heavier than the black.

Dharmaraja then declares: "E ma ho! Wonderful! Those who have obtained a human body, who delight in the sacred teachings, and fully realize their life's potential—as this achievement cannot be stolen or snatched by another's cleverness—will themselves find happiness through their own virtues. Such is the karma of the householder Laksmin Srījata. How wonderful it would be if all sentient beings were to do the same! Swiftly supplicate Avalokiteśvara, and instantly travel the white path!"

Then he says: "On the peak of Mount Potalaka in the east, I pray to the meditational deity Mahākāruṇika! Grant your blessing that we may realize the nature of mind to be the dharmakāya!" So he prays. He also makes the Aspirational Prayer Calling to the Buddhas and Bodhisattvas for Assistance.

Then he gives the formal introduction to obstructing the womb entrances during the bardo, and for binding the womb. Lastly, he confers the introduction to the methods of transferring consciousness to the pure buddha-fields. Immediately afterwards, the curtain is suddenly drawn back, and the form of Avalokiteśvara is vividly displayed. The householder Srījata is led along the white cotton path, clad in fine robes and wearing fine ornaments. Dharmaraja offers praise: "O child of my lineage, this is how one should serve the purpose of obtaining a human body. Rejoice in this!"
Then he makes three aspiration prayers of Avalokiteśvara:

"OṂ MAṆI PADME HŪṂ HRĪḤ!
May all sentient beings, including you Srījata,
Be guided on the path by the essence of the Six-syllable Mantra!
May cyclic existence with its three world-systems be churned to its depths,
And may the hells be exhausted and emptied!"
Thus he prays. He urges all to recite that Six-syllable Mantra.

Then the Guru says: "O children of my lineage, behold the distinction between the happiness and suffering experienced by the evil 'Red-handed Butcher' and the faithful householder Srījata, in consequence of their disparate accumulations of virtue and evil. As we ourselves shall also soon encounter such experiences, it is crucial that we strive after virtue and refrain from negativity!" So he speaks. Then he confers the empowerment, recites the auspicious verses, performs the aspirational prayer for the dedication of merit, followed by the concluding rite.

E ma! How wonderful! I, Padmākara, having directly seen the hells, composed this as an adjunct to the teaching of the Liberation by Hearing in the Intermediate States, in order to benefit sentient beings of the future. May it be encountered by those who are fortunate! This concludes the instruction revealing the natural expression of virtue and evil, called the Self-Liberation of Samsara. Śubham astu! May all be auspicious!
